video_title: Volts ϟ 7.1.2026 – Charlie Fisher + Ning Mosberger-Tang (A Key Arizona Race Will Test Clean...)
gist: Organizers Charlie Fisher (Arizonans for a Clean Economy) and Ning Mosberger-Tang (1.5 Degree Climate donor collaborative) explain how a disciplined, affordability-only turnout campaign flipped the obscure, land-owner-weighted Salt River Project utility board to a clean-energy majority, defeating Turning Point USA, and how they are attempting to scale that playbook to the statewide Arizona Corporation Commission and other under-the-radar utility-governance races (Georgia PUC, rural co-ops).

insights.claim: The Salt River Project (SRP), an Arizona public power utility, is regulated by no outside body — its elected board is the only check on the rates it charges and the plants it builds, making board elections the sole point of leverage over its plans for new gas plants, a new gas pipeline, and new rooftop-solar fees.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Self-regulated public utilities have no rate-case backstop, so the only investor/activist lever on their capex (gas plants, pipelines, solar fees) is the board election itself.
insights.quote: isn't regulated by anybody but itself

insights.claim: SRP board elections use an acreage-weighted franchise dating to pre-statehood: only eligible land owners may vote, votes are weighted by acres owned, and renters cannot vote at all.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A property-weighted electorate structurally biases utility governance toward large landholders, shaping who wins rate and generation fights.
insights.quote: Votes are weighted by the acre. The more land you have, the more votes you get. And renters don't vote at all.

insights.claim: SRP's power district governing board has 14 members plus an elected president and vice president, with the president, VP and 10 of the 14 members chosen on the acreage-based system, on four-year terms where half the body is up every other year, in elections held in April of even-numbered years.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: The off-cycle April timing and staggered terms are structural turnout suppressors that determine when a flip is even possible.
insights.quote: The elections are held in April of even numbered years when absolutely no one is thinking about elections.

insights.claim: SRP was created by early-1900s land owners, farmers and ranchers who pledged their land as collateral to convince the federal government to build Roosevelt Dam, which explains why the modern franchise remains tied to land ownership.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Understanding the historical origin clarifies why reform is hard — the voting rules are baked into the entity's founding charter.

insights.claim: Only 6,972 people voted in the 2022 SRP election out of roughly 750,000 eligible voters, for an entity delivering water and power to over two million people in central Arizona.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: A sub-1% turnout base means a few thousand mobilized votes can flip governance — the essence of a high-leverage 'low-hanging fruit' target.
insights.quote: 6,972 people voted in 2022

insights.claim: The campaign set an acreage cap of one acre or less to isolate voters likely to treat property as a home rather than a business or wealth-building investment, on the theory that owners of 10-plus acres view property as an investment and vote more conservatively.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Using land size as a proxy for persuadability is a novel micro-targeting model for property-weighted utility elections.
insights.quote: if you have an acre or less than an acre, there's a high probability that your home is is where you live and and that you will be persuaded

insights.claim: Arizonans for a Clean Economy ran a full multi-channel campaign into a targeted universe of about 60,000 voters, using five direct-mail pieces, digital ads, paid and volunteer canvassing, phone banking and texting.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Shows the concrete cost/effort footprint required to move an ultra-low-turnout race — a template for replicating the model elsewhere.
insights.quote: We threw the kitchen sink at this thing

insights.claim: The core turnout tactic was simply informing eligible voters the election existed and that they could request a mail ballot, exploiting SRP's permanent early-voter list even though Arizona has made mail-ballot requests harder in other elections.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: In obscure elections, awareness itself is the decisive variable — education work does most of the persuasion lifting.

insights.claim: The winning SRP message was purely about rates, not clean-versus-dirty energy: the campaign hammered that in February 2025 the SRP board voted to raise residential prices for the fifth time in five years, deliberately avoiding gas/pipeline framing.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Affordability, not decarbonization, is the persuasive frame in utility elections — a signal for how clean-energy campaigns should position going forward.
insights.quote: they voted to raise prices on residential customers for the fifth time in 5 years

insights.claim: SRP historically had higher favorability and lower rates than Arizona's investor-owned utilities, so the campaign had to actively puncture that goodwill by publicizing the repeated rate hikes.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: High incumbent favorability is a headwind that must be overcome with specific rate-hike facts, not generic anti-utility sentiment.

insights.claim: Roughly 80% of ACCE's SRP effort was purely educational C3 work; because a C3 cannot endorse candidates, a separate C4/PAC arm (Arizona PowerPack) followed up in the closing month to tell the same voters which candidates were 'the good guys'.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: The C3-education-plus-C4-endorsement handoff is the reusable legal architecture behind the win, and shows where donor money must flow.
insights.quote: so you can't weigh in on that basically

themes.theme: Turning Point USA and the backfire dynamic

insights.claim: Turning Point USA, founded by Charlie Kirk, poured major money into the spring SRP race backing the incumbent status-quo slate, but its high-profile involvement raised the race's salience and boosted turnout, which organizers and even GOP consultants judged to have backfired.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: In hidden low-turnout races, a well-funded opponent's attention can be self-defeating — nationalizing an obscure race helps the insurgents.

insights.claim: Turning Point canvassers wore shirts reading 'Thank you, Andy Biggs, for saving our utilities' and blanketed liberal Phoenix neighborhoods with anti-'green new deal' yard signs, messaging that organizers say confused and alienated voters.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Illustrates how nationalized culture-war messaging misfires in a technical, affordability-driven local race.
insights.quote: Thank you, Andy Biggs, for saving our utilities

insights.claim: Two SRP board members made sizable donations to Turning Point USA prior to its involvement — reportedly a $200,000 donation and a $20,000 donation — which Fisher suggests may partly explain why the national group entered the race despite official denials of a link.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Points to a donor-influence channel between regulated-entity board members and outside political groups worth scrutinizing.
insights.quote: not sure that passes the smell test for me

insights.claim: Fisher theorizes Turning Point used the SRP race as an early test of its field/ground-game operation ahead of November 2026, when its top Arizona priority is the governor's race and it is backing Congressman Andy Biggs in the Republican primary.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Signals that down-ballot utility races are becoming proving grounds for national field machines ahead of marquee 2026 contests.

themes.theme: Scaling to the Arizona Corporation Commission

insights.claim: The organizers are now targeting the Arizona Corporation Commission (ACC), a five-seat statewide utility regulator currently held entirely by Republicans, with two seats up in the November 2026 election.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Frames the concrete 2026 prize; winning both up seats still leaves a 3-2 GOP majority, so expectations must be calibrated.

insights.claim: Over 100,000 Arizona voters typically cast ballots for president, senate or governor but skip the Corporation Commission line entirely, so reducing that down-ballot drop-off is a central strategic objective.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Ballot roll-off, not persuasion, may decide the ACC race — turnout completion is the key metric to watch.
insights.quote: you cannot skip this election and you have two votes

insights.claim: Unlike the facially nonpartisan SRP race, ACC candidates appear on the ballot with party labels, and Arizona has more registered Republicans than Democrats, so organizers plan to target independents — the second-largest and nearly co-equal bloc — rather than fight on partisanship.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Partisan labeling reintroduces a registration disadvantage; the swing independent bloc is the decisive target universe.

insights.claim: Democrats went after the Arizona Corporation Commission in both 2022 (a strong year where they won governor, secretary of state and attorney general) and 2024 and lost all ACC races both times, making the seat something of a persistent blind spot.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The two-cycle losing streak sets a high bar of skepticism; the case for 2026 being different rests on coalition scale and environment.
insights.quote: There does seem something slightly cursed about this.

insights.claim: The 2026 clean-energy coalition wrote a single shared plan in May, using one shared voter universe, shared research and polling across C3s, C4s and IE groups focused solely on the ACC race — a level of integration organizers say exceeds the looser coordination of prior cycles.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Operational integration is the organizers' stated edge over the 2022/2024 losses — the variable to watch for whether this cycle differs.
insights.quote: We got in the room in May and wrote one plan.

insights.claim: ACCE launched an ACC-focused education campaign called 'Follow the Power' to teach voters that the commission regulates monopoly utilities and directly affects monthly bills, and to drive testimony and turnout.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Named early-education infrastructure is the leading indicator of the campaign's seriousness and reach.

insights.claim: Both Democratic ACC candidates and reportedly all three Republican candidates are running through Arizona's clean-elections public-funding system, which caps them at about $29,000 in $200 seed increments, roughly $100,000 for the primary and about $240,000 for the general.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Because candidates are spending-capped, the bulk of messaging and organizing money must flow through outside groups — that is where the real budget battle sits.

insights.claim: In 2024 the ACC clean-energy candidates pledged to refuse PAC money and take only public funds, which left them massively outspent; in 2026 the coalition is going 'gloves off' with PAC and outside money to make it a fairer money fight.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Abandoning the no-PAC pledge is a concrete change from the losing 2024 approach and a reason the 2026 outcome could diverge.
insights.quote: kind of resulted in them getting outspent a gazillion to one

insights.claim: Arizona's Prop 211 forces C4 groups to disclose their donors, which Mosberger-Tang says makes some anonymity-seeking donors more cautious about investing in Arizona but has not been a major deterrent for her network, which donated to the disclosed SRP PAC directly.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: Donor-disclosure rules are a real friction on outside spending strategy that both sides must navigate in Arizona.

themes.theme: Data centers as an affordability and clean-energy wedge

insights.claim: The Arizona legislature just removed a long-standing data-center tax credit, and both parties immediately claimed credit, signaling that being 'tough on data centers' is becoming a bipartisan competitive stance like affordability.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Data-center politics are converging with affordability as a top voter issue, reshaping incentives and subsidies that hyperscalers and utilities rely on.

insights.claim: ACC Republican incumbent Kevin Thompson works as a lobbyist for data-center firm Edgecore, which Fisher argues undercuts any Republican attempt to run as anti-data-center.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: A regulator's direct industry ties are an exploitable vulnerability and a watch-item for conflict-of-interest attacks in the race.
insights.quote: one of the incumbents Kevin Thompson literally is a lobbyist for data centers

insights.claim: 1.5 Degree Climate treats data-center buildout as likely unstoppable and reframes it as a clean-energy opportunity, pushing state policies (in Illinois, Colorado and Virginia) requiring data centers to bring their own clean energy and making hyperscalers cover the transmission and load-addition costs rather than shifting them onto consumers.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: A cost-allocation policy push that would force hyperscalers to internalize grid costs — directly material to data-center economics and utility ratepayers.
insights.quote: make sure that the data centers will be clean bring their own energy

insights.claim: Mosberger-Tang argues that if data centers are overbuilt — plausible given far more buildout requests than can currently be used — the excess clean generation could become a future energy source supporting broad electrification.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: A contrarian upside case on data-center overbuild that runs against the populist 'data centers are bad' consensus.
insights.quote: there are a lot more uh requests for data centers build out than what they actually can use right now

themes.theme: Donor network and under-the-radar utility races

insights.claim: Ning Mosberger-Tang founded the 1.5 Degree Climate donor collaborative in March 2022; it has grown from one member (herself) to more than 700 and says it has moved more than $36 billion through collective effort to over 100 organizations in four years.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of an organized climate donor-vetting machine steering capital to high-leverage electoral targets.
insights.quote: in the past four years we have moved more than 36 billion dollars through our collective effort

insights.claim: 1.5 Degree Climate's investment thesis is to identify 'low-hanging fruit, high impact but under the radar' elections where turning out a small number of voters can flip control, and to route vetted donor funds there.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: This is the explicit capital-allocation model — obscurity plus low turnout equals outsized influence per dollar.
insights.quote: We are trying to identify lowhanging fruit, high impact but under the radar strategies

insights.claim: The network was heavily involved in the 2024 Georgia Public Service Commission wins (backing Georgia Conservation Voters' Bante Makoko and candidates Peter Hubbard and Alicia Johnson) and plans a two-cycle path — holding one seat and winning another in November — to reach a 3-2 majority, with Hubbard needing reelection and Sheila Edwards running for an open seat.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: Georgia is the proven template for the Arizona ACC two-election majority strategy — a live comparable to track.

insights.claim: Rural electric co-op board elections, which typically run below 10% (often near 5%) turnout and govern notoriously fossil-heavy, conservative utilities, are a next frontier; 1.5 Degree Climate is backing CVIQ pilots using digital ads to reach and educate co-op voters in Midwestern states.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: Flags rural co-op boards as the next high-leverage, low-turnout clean-energy governance target.
insights.quote: the turnout rate is like 5% you know below 10%

insights.claim: 1.5 Degree Climate is prioritizing state 'trifecta' wins in Wisconsin, Michigan, Nevada and Arizona in 2026, framing them as consequential for redistricting, governance and election administration in 2028 battleground states, not just clean energy.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: Maps where organized climate donor money is concentrating in 2026 and why the stakes extend to redistricting and 2028.

insights.claim: Fisher argues even a single pro-clean-energy ACC commissioner has outsized value because each commissioner holds individual subpoena power to open utilities' books and extract data they are reluctant to share, and that some Republican commissioners are winnable coalition partners rather than committed opponents.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Minority representation still yields real oversight leverage — a reason the race matters even without a majority.
insights.quote: every commissioner has individual subpoena power

insights.claim: The strategic aim of 2026 is to build infrastructure, voter lists and education for a real shot at a pro-clean-energy ACC majority in 2028, when three seats are up and the coalition would need to win just one more after holding two.
insights.speaker: Charlie Fisher
insights.so_what: Reframes 2026 as a setup year; the actual majority payoff is a 2028 bet, mirroring the Georgia two-cycle model.
insights.quote: an opportunity to elect a proclaim majority for the first time in decades

insights.claim: Mosberger-Tang expects a more favorable 2026 election environment than 2022 or 2024, arguing federal-government overreach has produced a backlash that mobilizes voters and that affordability concerns now align with the clean-energy message.
insights.speaker: Ning Mosberger-Tang
insights.so_what: The organizers' bull case rests on environment plus affordability alignment — the macro assumptions to test against actual results.
insights.quote: clean energy is not the cheap energy

insights.claim: Both organizers frame the Arizona ACC race as a national bellwether for whether the affordability-plus-anti-incumbent message and a coordinated clean-energy electoral machine can win nationalized, previously obscure utility-governance fights.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Positions the November 2026 ACC result as a read-through signal for similar clean-energy utility races across other states.
insights.quote: the ACC race will be kind of a bellweather of a lot of other things
